about.title: About Daris
about.description: Structured, progressive online Islamic education, in the methodology of Al-Azhar Al-Sharif.
about.story1: Daris is an online platform for students who want serious Islamic study. Quran, Arabic, and fiqh, taught the Al-Azhar way.
about.story2: There are plenty of Arabic flashcard apps and Quran recitation channels online. What's harder to find is the thing traditional Islamic study has always needed: a qualified teacher who listens to you read, catches the mistakes you can't hear yourself make, and walks you through real material in the right order. That's what Daris is, online.
about.story3: The methodology follows Al-Azhar Al-Sharif, the institution in Cairo whose curriculum still sets the standard for classical Islamic scholarship. Tajwid before memorisation. Grammar before reading. Hanafi fiqh grounded in recognised texts rather than loose opinion.
about.story4: Lessons are live. Quran and Arabic are usually close, corrective teaching where the teacher hears every recitation or reading as it happens. Fiqh is usually taught in a group, which fits the nature of the material. A beginner working on letter articulation and an advanced student polishing recitation use the same platform but follow very different plans.
about.story5: Three programs cover most of what students come looking for. Quran, with tajwid, tafsir, recitation, and memorisation, taught in English or Arabic. Classical Arabic for non-native speakers. Fiqh and aqeedah taught in Arabic, on the Hanafi madhhab, including the stories of the prophets and the 40 Hadith of an-Nawawi. Lessons are scheduled in the student's timezone, and English support is available when Arabic alone would slow the student down.
about.methodologyIntro: Every Daris lesson runs on the same small set of principles. They're not optional extras. They're the reason the work produces real understanding instead of surface progress.
about.method1Body: Every program follows a documented sequence. A student starting Quran begins with letter articulation (makhārij al-ḥurūf), works through the rules of tajwid in order, and only then builds toward extended memorisation. No skipping steps.
about.method2Body: A teacher listens in real time. Mistakes get caught the same day they happen, before weeks of repetition embed them.
about.method3Body: Arabic grammar is taught from traditional naḥw texts. Fiqh follows recognised Hanafi manuals. Students know what they're reading and why. Every curriculum choice has a source you can point to.
about.method4Body: Lessons move at the student's pace. A beginner isn't pushed ahead, and an advanced student isn't held back. Depth adapts without dropping the standard.
about.method5Body: Instruction happens in Arabic when the material needs it (Fiqh and aqeedah always). English support is available for students whose Arabic is still developing, and for Quran or Arabic lessons where it helps.
about.philosophyText: Knowledge connected with practice. A pace that fits the week you actually have, not the one you wish you had.
about.langText: Arabic for text and recitation. English support available through the Daris team. Students welcome from any country.
about.credStudentsVal: Men and children, beginners to advanced
about.ctaText: Share your goals and timezone and we'll guide you from there.

programs.title: Quran, Arabic & Fiqh
programs.description: Take one program or combine them based on your goals.
programs.quranNote: Beginners through to advanced. Available in English and Arabic, for men and children.
programs.arabicNote: Build confidence with classical texts at your pace.
programs.fiqhAdultsItems: Fiqh, aqeedah, hadith, mustalah al-hadith, and ulum al-Qur'an.
programs.fiqhChildrenItems: Stories of the prophets, the Prophetic seerah, lives of the noble companions, fiqh, and the 40 Hadith of an-Nawawi.
programs.fiqhNote: Taught in Arabic. Structured lessons with recognised texts.
programs.quranSyllabus: Absolute beginners start with makhārij al-ḥurūf, the correct articulation of each Arabic letter, before touching a single verse. Once articulation is reliable, the tajwid rules follow in traditional order: noon and meem sakin, madd, qalqala, the rules of ra and lam, and wakf. Recitation is paced against the student's current level. Memorisation, when it's part of the plan, is built on top of secure tajwid rather than alongside it. Quran study is available in both English and Arabic.
programs.arabicSyllabus: The Arabic program is built for non-native speakers, particularly those who want to engage with the Quran and classical texts rather than hold a modern conversation. Grammar-first: letter and word forms, then the basics of iʿrāb, then verb patterns, then sentence structure. Vocabulary comes from the Quran and recognised classical readers, not contemporary lists. Once the foundation is set, students read short classical passages with live correction, then move to longer texts at a pace that keeps accuracy ahead of breadth.
programs.fiqhSyllabus: Fiqh is taught in Arabic, on the Hanafi madhhab, using recognised manuals section by section: purity, prayer, fasting, zakat, hajj, and related topics. Aqeedah sits alongside. For adults the track runs in small groups (which suits the material); a solo option is available for students who prefer it. For children the shape is different: stories of the prophets and the Prophetic seerah build the narrative foundation, the 40 Hadith of an-Nawawi builds memorised reference points, and an age-appropriate fiqh introduction sits alongside.
programs.startText: Message Daris with your goals, current level, how many lessons per week you're aiming for, and your timezone. A short assessment sets the entry point, and lessons begin once an appropriate plan is agreed.

articles.title: Writing on Quran, Arabic and fiqh

faq.title: Frequently asked questions
faq.description: Short answers to the questions students and parents ask most often before starting at Daris.
faq.q1: What does a Daris lesson look like?
faq.a1: It depends on the subject. Quran students read aloud and the teacher corrects tajwid and articulation as it happens, working through a structured plan. Arabic runs the same way: grammar and reading with live correction. Fiqh is usually taught in a small group, in Arabic, using a recognised Hanafi manual. Every lesson is live and scheduled in the student's timezone.
faq.q2: How is Daris different from a Quran app or YouTube channel?
faq.a2: Apps drill vocabulary. Channels broadcast recitation. Neither replaces the part of Islamic study that has always needed a teacher: someone who hears you read, catches the mistake you can't hear yourself, explains what a classical text actually means, and adjusts your curriculum based on what you're struggling with this week. That's what Daris is built around.
faq.q3: Do you teach beginners, or only advanced students?
faq.a3: Both. Absolute beginners working on the correct articulation of Arabic letters are welcome, and so are advanced students polishing recitation at a serious level. Order and depth adapt to the student. The structure doesn't change.
faq.a4: Yes. Children have their own curriculum path: shorter lessons, age-appropriate material (stories of the prophets, the Prophetic seerah, the 40 Hadith of an-Nawawi), and a pace that respects attention span without dropping the standard of what's being learned.
faq.a5: An ijazah is a traditional certification from a qualified teacher that the student has recited the Quran (or read a specific text) with correct tajwid and without omissions or additions. It carries a sanad, a chain of transmission traceable through teachers over centuries. The Daris Quran track is built on the traditional sequence that leads in that direction. Whether a specific ijazah path is right for a given student is a conversation to have once the teacher has assessed their level.
faq.q6: Is the instruction in English or Arabic?
faq.a6: Quran and tajwid are available in English and Arabic. Fiqh and aqeedah are taught in Arabic only, which is how the tradition keeps its texts accurate. Arabic lessons are conducted in Arabic with English support where it helps, especially early on. General communication and scheduling can happen in either language.
faq.a7: Any. Lessons are scheduled in the student's local time. The student picks a weekly slot that fits their schedule, and that slot stays the slot unless both sides agree to move it.
faq.q8: What do I need to attend a lesson?
faq.a8: A reliable internet connection and a device with a microphone. Video is strongly preferred so the teacher can see the student's mouth position for tajwid correction. Nothing special to install. Lessons run over widely available video-call tools.
faq.a9: Yes. Many students take Quran alongside Arabic, or Arabic alongside fiqh. The weekly schedule is built from the student's goals: one program, two, or all three. No packaged tiers to fit into.
faq.a10: Message Daris via WhatsApp or email with your goals, current level, how many lessons a week you'd like, and your timezone. A short assessment sets the entry point. Lessons begin once a plan is agreed.
faq.ctaText: Message Daris directly. We reply within a few working days.

admin.suspendConfirm: Are you sure you want to suspend this student?

admin.deleteAllConfirm: Are you sure you want to delete ALL classes? This cannot be undone.
admin.timezoneHint: The time above is interpreted in this timezone, no matter where you're scheduling from.

teachers.title: Teachers
teachers.subtitle: Promote enrolled students to teachers and view their assigned-student rosters. A teacher can also be a student — senior students who help teach keep both capabilities.
teachers.subtitleTeacher: Other teachers on the team. You can see names and student counts; rosters are private.
teachers.emptyHint: Teachers are added by the site owner.
teachers.emptyHintSheikh: Promote an enrolled student using the button above to add the first teacher.
teachers.promoteSubtitle: Pick the enrolled students who should also be able to teach. They keep all their student-side data.
teachers.promoteSubtitlePure: These users will become teachers only — they won't appear in the students list and can't be assigned to attend classes.
teachers.noPromoteCandidates: No eligible students. Either everyone is already a teacher, or no students are enrolled yet.
teachers.pureTeacherHint: Removes them from the students list and the schedule form's student picker. Use for hired teachers who don't take classes themselves. Demoting them later restores their student status automatically.
teachers.demoteConfirm: Remove the teacher role from {name}? Their {count} student assignments will be removed. They keep their own classes as a student.
teachers.dualRoleTooltip: This teacher is also an enrolled student and attends classes themselves.
teachers.pureTeacherTooltip: This is a pure teacher and doesn't attend classes themselves.
teachers.ownerTooltip: The sheikh — runs the school and teaches. Auto-default teacher when a student has no explicit assignment.

gcal.title: Google Calendar + Sheets
gcal.subtitleNotConnected: Connect your Google account so every class becomes a calendar event with its own Meet link, and per-student notebooks save to your Drive.
gcal.subtitleActive: Calendar events + Meet links sync automatically. Per-student notebooks save to your Drive.
gcal.subtitleNeedsReconnect: We can no longer reach your Google account. Reconnect to resume syncing.
gcal.subtitleDisconnected: Sync is off. New classes fall back to the global meeting link below.
gcal.notConfigured: Google Calendar is not configured on this server. Set GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID, GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET, GOOGLE_OAUTH_REDIRECT_URI, and TOKEN_ENCRYPTION_KEY in the environment to enable it.
gcal.notConnectedHint: One-time setup. You'll be redirected to Google to grant access to events you own — no read access to other calendars.
gcal.disconnectConfirm: Disconnect your Google Calendar? Existing events stay on your calendar; new classes will fall back to the global meeting link until you reconnect.
gcal.needsReconnectHint: Your Google credentials were rejected (revoked, password changed, or session expired).
gcal.disconnectedHint: You disconnected this earlier. Events created while connected are still on your Google Calendar.
gcal.stuckSyncHint: {count} class(es) failed to sync to your calendar after retries. They'll auto-retry within 5 minutes, or click below to retry now.
gcal.studentsFolderHint: Per-student notebooks save to your Drive at Daris Students/.
gcal.needsScopeUpgrade: Reconnect Google to enable per-student notebooks (one-time permission upgrade).

notebook.errNoConnection: Connect Google Calendar first, then create the notebook.
notebook.errNeedsScopeUpgrade: Reconnect Google to grant Drive + Sheets permissions, then try again.
